rationale: Art.20(1) requires management bodies to approve cybersecurity risk-management measures; ISO 27001 Cl.5.1 requires top management to demonstrate leadership and commitment, including establishing the information security policy.

Art.20(1) requires management bodies to oversee implementation of Art.21 measures; ISO 27001 Cl.9.3 mandates formal management review at planned intervals covering ISMS performance, which is broader than oversight of individual risk measures.

Art.20(1) imposes legal liability on management body members for infringements; A.5.2 assigns information security roles and responsibilities without imposing personal legal liability — the accountability scope differs.

Art.20(2) specifically requires members of management bodies to follow cybersecurity training to identify risks and assess practices; A.6.3 addresses awareness, education, and training for all personnel, making Art.20(2) a specific subset targeting the governance layer.

Art.20(2) encourages entities to offer similar training to employees on a regular basis; A.6.3 mandates that all persons performing activities relevant to the ISMS receive appropriate security awareness, education, and training — near co-extensive at this sub-point.

Art.21(1) requires proportionate technical and organisational measures based on a risk-analysis considering severity and likelihood; ISO 27001 Cl.6.1.1 requires planning actions to address risks and opportunities that considers the ISMS context.

Art.21(1) mandates keeping measures at 'state of the art'; ISO 27001 Cl.10.2 requires continual improvement of the ISMS suitability, adequacy, and effectiveness — the Art.21(1) requirement is more outcome-specific regarding technology currency.

Art.21(2)(a) mandates risk analysis as a cybersecurity measure; ISO 27001 Cl.6.1.2 requires a formal information security risk assessment process identifying, analysing, and evaluating risks — the sub-requirements are co-extensive.

Art.21(2)(a) explicitly requires information security policies as a minimum measure; A.5.1 mandates policies for information security that are defined, approved, published, communicated, and reviewed — the sub-point is co-extensive.

Art.21(2)(b) requires incident handling as a cybersecurity measure; A.5.24 mandates incident management planning and preparation defining roles, responsibilities, and procedures for handling information security incidents.

Art.21(2)(b) incident handling implies detection and triage; A.5.25 requires that information security events are assessed and a decision made whether to classify them as incidents — the sub-aspect is co-extensive.
Art.21(2)(b) incident handling requires response capability; A.5.26 requires a structured response to confirmed information security incidents including containment, communication, and resolution.
Art.21(2)(b) incident handling includes post-incident review; A.5.27 requires learning from information security incidents and feeding findings back into ISMS improvements.

Art.21(2)(b) incident handling requires preserving information to support investigation; A.5.28 explicitly covers collection of evidence during and after incidents to support investigation and legal or regulatory reporting.

Art.21(2)(b) incident handling presupposes detection via monitoring; A.8.16 mandates monitoring of networks, systems, and applications to detect anomalous behaviour, making the NIS2 sub-aspect a consumer of the broader ISO monitoring control.

Art.21(2)(c) explicitly lists backup as a business continuity measure; A.8.13 requires that backup copies of information and software are maintained and regularly tested in accordance with the agreed backup policy.
Art.21(2)(c) requires disaster recovery as part of BCM; A.5.30 mandates ICT readiness for business continuity, ensuring IT services can be recovered to required levels within required timeframes.
Art.21(2)(c) requires business continuity management including crisis management; A.5.29 mandates maintaining appropriate information security even when normal operations are disrupted.

Art.21(2)(c) crisis management covers broader organisational response beyond IT; ISO 27001 Cl.8.1 requires planning and implementing processes to meet information security requirements — IT-focused, not the full crisis management scope required by NIS2.

Art.21(2)(d) explicitly addresses security in the supply chain between entities and their direct suppliers and service providers, especially for ICT; A.5.21 directly covers managing information security in the ICT supply chain.

Art.21(2)(d) covers securing agreements and practices with direct suppliers; A.5.19 broadly covers information security in supplier relationships including agreed security policies, making NIS2 a specific instance of the broader ISO requirement.

Art.21(2)(d) requires addressing security within supplier agreements; A.5.20 mandates that relevant information security requirements are established and agreed with each supplier that may access, process, or store the organisation's information.

Art.21(2)(d) requires monitoring supplier security posture; A.5.22 mandates regular monitoring, review, and change management of supplier services to ensure agreed information security levels are maintained.
Art.21(2)(d) applies supply chain security to cloud service providers; A.5.23 directly addresses information security for the acquisition, use, management, and exit from cloud services.
Art.21(2)(e) covers security in network acquisition and maintenance; A.8.20 requires networks to be secured, managed, and controlled to protect information in systems and applications.

Art.21(2)(e) requires security in information systems development and maintenance; A.8.25 mandates a secure development life cycle establishing and applying rules for the secure development of software and systems.
Art.21(2)(e) covers security in system acquisition; A.8.26 requires that information security requirements be identified, specified, and approved when acquiring applications.

Art.21(2)(e) explicitly requires vulnerability handling and disclosure policies; A.8.8 mandates obtaining information about technical vulnerabilities, evaluating exposure, and taking appropriate measures — directly co-extensive.

Art.21(2)(e) system maintenance security includes secure development practices; A.8.28 specifically addresses secure coding principles to prevent the introduction of vulnerabilities during development.
Art.21(2)(e) system development security requires testing security measures; A.8.29 mandates security testing in development and acceptance to verify security requirements are met before deployment.
Art.21(2)(e) system maintenance security includes change procedures; A.8.32 mandates change management procedures for information processing facilities and information systems.

Art.21(2)(e) system acquisition and development security includes architecture; A.8.27 requires secure system architecture and engineering principles to be established and applied during system development.

Art.21(2)(f) requires policies and procedures to assess the effectiveness of cybersecurity risk-management measures; ISO 27001 Cl.9.1 mandates monitoring, measurement, analysis, and evaluation of information security performance.

Art.21(2)(f) effectiveness assessment may include independent review; A.5.35 mandates that the organisation's approach to managing information security is reviewed independently at planned intervals or when significant changes occur.
Art.21(2)(f) requires procedures to assess compliance with risk-management measures; A.5.36 mandates compliance with information security policies, rules, and standards for information security.

Art.21(2)(f) effectiveness assessment procedures include internal audit; ISO 27001 Cl.9.2 explicitly requires planned internal audits to determine whether the ISMS conforms to requirements and is effectively implemented.

Art.21(2)(g) basic cyber hygiene includes strong password/passphrase management; A.5.17 covers management of authentication information broadly including passwords, tokens, and certificates — the NIS2 sub-point is a specific instance.

Art.21(2)(g) basic cyber hygiene includes keeping software updated and patching; A.8.8 addresses management of technical vulnerabilities including obtaining information and taking timely remedial actions — patch management is a key sub-aspect.

Art.21(2)(g) basic cyber hygiene includes anti-malware controls on endpoint devices; A.8.7 requires that controls to detect, prevent, and recover from malware are implemented, backed by appropriate user awareness.

Art.21(2)(g) basic cyber hygiene includes clear desk and screen lock policies; A.7.7 explicitly mandates a clear desk policy for papers and removable storage media and a clear screen policy for information processing facilities.
Art.21(2)(g) basic cyber hygiene includes secure browsing practices; A.8.23 requires web filtering to manage access to external websites and reduce exposure to malicious content.

Art.21(2)(g) basic cyber hygiene covers securing endpoint devices including updates and screen locks; A.8.1 addresses protection of information accessed via, stored on, or processed by user endpoint devices.

Art.21(2)(g) cybersecurity training component requires regular training for all employees; A.6.3 mandates information security awareness, education, and training for all persons performing relevant activities — directly co-extensive.

Art.21(2)(g) requires specialised training for high-risk roles such as sysadmins and developers; A.6.3 addresses training at an organisation-wide level without explicitly distinguishing role-specific depth requirements — the NIS2 sub-point is more granular.

Art.21(2)(h) explicitly requires policies and procedures on the use of cryptography and encryption; A.8.24 mandates rules for the effective use of cryptography to protect information — near-identical in scope at this sub-point.

Art.21(2)(h) encryption policies include protecting data in transit; A.5.14 addresses information transfer controls including cryptographic protection of transmitted data — the NIS2 sub-aspect is one of several mechanisms covered.
Art.21(2)(h) encryption policies include protecting data at rest; A.8.24 covers the use of cryptography to protect confidentiality, authenticity, and integrity of information including stored data.

Art.21(2)(h) encryption policy implies cryptographic key management; A.8.24 requires rules on key management including generation, storage, archiving, retrieval, distribution, retirement, and destruction of cryptographic keys.
Art.21(2)(i) human resources security includes background checks/screening; A.6.1 mandates that background verification checks on all candidates for employment are carried out prior to joining.

Art.21(2)(i) human resources security requires security obligations communicated to staff; A.6.2 mandates that employment terms and conditions state the personnel's and organisation's responsibilities for information security.

Art.21(2)(i) human resources security includes procedures for personnel departure; A.6.5 mandates that information security responsibilities and duties that remain valid after termination or change of employment are defined and communicated.

Art.21(2)(i) access control requires policies governing access to systems and data; A.5.15 mandates rules to control physical and logical access to information and other associated assets based on business and information security requirements.

Art.21(2)(i) access control includes least-privilege and RBAC; A.8.2 requires that the allocation and use of privileged access rights be restricted and managed, supporting least-privilege enforcement.
Art.21(2)(i) access control encompasses identity management; A.5.16 covers the full lifecycle of digital identities to enable correct identification and authentication of persons and systems.

Art.21(2)(i) access control includes granting and revoking access rights; A.5.18 requires that access rights to assets are provisioned, reviewed, modified, and removed in accordance with the access control policy.

Art.21(2)(i) access control includes restricting access to information; A.8.3 requires that access to information and application system functions is restricted in accordance with the access control policy.
Art.21(2)(i) asset management requires maintaining an asset inventory; A.5.9 mandates that an inventory of information and other associated assets, with owners, is developed and maintained.

Art.21(2)(i) asset management includes rules for asset use; A.5.10 requires that rules for acceptable use and procedures for handling information and other assets are identified, documented, and implemented.

Art.21(2)(i) asset management includes classifying assets by sensitivity; A.5.12 mandates that information is classified to enable appropriate protection according to its confidentiality, integrity, and availability requirements.

Art.21(2)(i) human resources security combined with asset management requires return of assets upon termination; A.5.11 mandates that all employees and external party users return all organisational assets in their possession upon termination of employment.

Art.21(2)(j) explicitly requires multi-factor authentication or continuous authentication solutions; A.8.5 mandates secure authentication technologies and procedures based on access restrictions, making MFA the primary technical control.

Art.21(2)(j) MFA requirement applies especially to privileged and administrative accounts; A.8.2 covers management of privileged access rights and implicitly requires strong authentication — NIS2 is more explicit on MFA as a mechanism.

Art.21(2)(j) requires secured voice, video, and text communications; A.5.14 addresses protection of information in transit including agreements, technical controls, and cryptographic protection for all forms of communication transfer.

Art.21(2)(j) requires secured emergency communication systems for crisis scenarios; A.5.29 covers maintaining information security during disruption but does not specifically mandate secure out-of-band emergency communication channels.

Art.21(2)(j) specifies continuous authentication as an alternative to MFA, which is a more advanced zero-trust capability; A.8.5 covers secure authentication broadly but does not specify continuous or adaptive authentication — NIS2 is more prescriptive here.

Art.23(1) requires a 24-hour early warning to CSIRT/competent authority for significant incidents; A.5.24 covers incident management planning and preparation but does not specify regulatory reporting timelines to external authorities.

Art.23(1) requires a 72-hour incident notification to competent authority with initial assessment; A.5.26 addresses internal incident response but does not mandate external regulatory reporting within prescribed timelines.

Art.23(1) requires a 1-month final report with root cause, impact, and applied measures; A.5.27 covers learning from incidents and feeding lessons back into the ISMS, which is a related but internally-focused process without the regulatory reporting obligation.

Art.23(2) defines when an incident is 'significant' requiring reporting (severe operational disruption or financial loss); A.5.25 requires assessment and decision-making on information security events to classify them as incidents, which parallels the significance determination.
Art.23(3) requires establishing contacts with competent national authorities and CSIRTs for incident reporting; A.5.5 mandates maintaining appropriate contacts with relevant authorities.

Art.23(4) requires parallel notification to every affected Member State's CSIRT for cross-border significant incidents; A.5.5 covers authority contacts generically and does not address cross-border regulatory coordination obligations.

Art.21(2)(a) information security policy implies selecting and applying risk treatments; ISO 27001 Cl.6.1.3 requires determining and applying risk treatment options including the Annex A controls and producing a risk treatment plan.

Art.21(2)(a) risk analysis and security policy establishment aligns with documenting applicable controls; ISO 27001 Cl.6.1.3 requires producing a Statement of Applicability documenting selected Annex A controls and their justification.

Art.21(2)(a) risk analysis requires identifying who is responsible for managing identified risks; ISO 27001 Cl.6.1.2 explicitly requires identifying risk owners for each assessed information security risk.

Art.21(2)(c) BCM and disaster recovery require redundant processing capabilities; A.8.14 mandates that redundancy of information processing facilities is implemented to meet availability requirements.

Art.21(2)(e) security in network acquisition includes securing network services; A.8.21 requires that security mechanisms, service levels, and management requirements of all network services are identified and included in agreements.
Art.21(2)(e) network security in maintenance includes network segmentation; A.8.22 mandates that groups of information services, users, and systems are segregated in the organisation's networks.
Art.21(2)(e) covers security in system acquisition including outsourced development; A.8.30 requires that the organisation supervises and monitors the activity of outsourced system development.

Art.21(2)(e) system development security requires separating environments; A.8.31 requires separation of development, test, and production environments to reduce risks of unauthorised access or changes.

Art.21(2)(i) access control implicitly covers protecting development assets including source code; A.8.4 specifically requires that read and write access to source code, development tools, and software libraries are appropriately managed.

Art.21(2)(i) asset management together with classification implies labelling; A.5.13 requires that an appropriate set of procedures for information labelling is developed and implemented in accordance with the classification scheme.

Art.21(2)(g) cyber hygiene practices implicitly include preventing unauthorised data exfiltration; A.8.12 mandates data leakage prevention measures to detect and prevent the unauthorised disclosure of information.

Art.21(2)(i) human resources security includes confidentiality obligations for personnel; A.6.6 requires that persons under the organisation's control sign confidentiality or non-disclosure agreements reflecting the organisation's needs for information protection.
Art.21(2)(i) human resources security and access control encompasses remote work security; A.6.7 mandates that security measures are implemented when personnel are working remotely.

Art.21(2)(g) cyber hygiene training includes training staff to report security events; A.6.8 requires that the organisation provides a mechanism for personnel to report observed or suspected information security events.

Art.21(2)(b) incident handling requires capturing incident information; A.8.15 mandates that logs recording user activities, exceptions, faults, and information security events are produced, stored, protected, and analysed.

Art.21(2)(f) procedures to assess effectiveness require metrics and KPIs; ISO 27001 Cl.9.1 requires the organisation to determine what needs to be monitored and measured, methods, and when results will be analysed and evaluated.

Art.21(2)(c) disaster recovery requires regular testing of backup restoration; A.8.13 requires that backup copies of information are regularly tested to ensure they can be relied upon when needed in a disaster recovery situation.

Art.21(2)(e) network and system maintenance security includes secure configuration management; A.8.9 mandates that configurations, including security configurations, of hardware, software, services, and networks are established, documented, implemented, monitored, and reviewed.

Art.21(2)(d) supply chain security includes third-party development relationships; A.8.30 requires supervision of outsourced system development which overlaps with NIS2 supply chain ICT security but from the development security angle.

Art.21(2)(a) risk analysis and security policies require documentation; ISO 27001 Cl.7.5 mandates that the ISMS includes all documented information required by the standard, which encompasses risk assessment records, treatment plans, and policy documents.

Art.21(2)(f) assessment procedures include corrective actions for identified gaps; ISO 27001 Cl.10.1 (2022: Cl.10.2) requires that when nonconformity occurs the organisation reacts to it and takes corrective action.

Art.23(1) imposes specific 24h/72h/1-month regulatory reporting timelines to external authorities that go beyond what A.5.24 requires; A.5.24 covers planning for incident management but is oriented toward internal procedures — the external notification obligation is a NIS2-specific addition.

Art.20(1) assigns accountability for cybersecurity measures to the management body as a whole; ISO 27001 Cl.5.3 requires top management to assign responsibility and authority for roles relevant to information security including communicating within the organisation.

Art.21(2)(i) human resources security includes consequences for policy violations; A.6.4 requires that a formal and communicated disciplinary process exists and is initiated against personnel who commit an information security breach.
Art.21(2)(c) BCM requires regularly testing continuity plans; A.5.30 mandates ICT readiness for business continuity including testing and verification that ICT continuity objectives can be met.

Art.21(2)(e) system maintenance security requires controlling software installed on production systems; A.8.19 mandates that procedures and measures are implemented to securely manage software installation on operational systems.

Art.21(2)(h) policies on cryptography include securing communications channels; A.8.24 covers use of cryptography for all information protection purposes including transit, rest, and communications — the NIS2 sub-point is one application area.

Art.21(2)(i) asset management includes secure disposal of information-bearing assets; A.7.14 mandates that items of equipment containing storage media are verified to ensure that any sensitive data has been removed or securely overwritten prior to disposal or reuse.

Art.21(2)(g) basic cyber hygiene practices include ensuring data is backed up as a foundational practice; A.8.13 formalises backup requirements — the relationship is that NIS2 treats backup as a hygiene baseline while ISO addresses it as a formal control.

Art.21(2)(j) secured voice, video, and text communications implies end-to-end encryption; A.8.24 use of cryptography covers technical measures to ensure confidentiality and integrity of communications.

Art.21(1) proportionate risk management requires management actively ensuring compliance; A.5.4 mandates that management requires all personnel to apply information security in accordance with established policies — the obligation to foster a security culture is related.

Art.21(2)(e) explicitly mentions vulnerability disclosure policies for self-discovered vulnerabilities; A.8.8 addresses handling of technical vulnerabilities including the process for evaluation and disclosure.

Art.21(2)(b) incident handling requires classifying severity and impact of events; A.5.25 mandates assessment of information security events and decision-making on whether to classify them as incidents.

Art.21(2)(d) supply chain security requires assessing risks from direct suppliers; ISO 27001 Cl.6.1.2 covers the full information security risk assessment process across all risk domains including supply chain — NIS2 is a specific application.

Art.21(2)(c) BCM and continuity planning must account for regulatory requirements; A.5.31 requires a maintained understanding of legal, statutory, regulatory, and contractual requirements applicable to information security.

Art.21(2)(f) effectiveness assessment procedures benefit from threat intelligence to evaluate whether measures address current threats; A.5.7 mandates collection and analysis of information about threats relevant to the organisation to inform security decisions.

Art.21(2)(a) risk analysis and security policies require defining the scope of what is protected; ISO 27001 Cl.4.3 requires the organisation to determine the boundaries and applicability of the ISMS to establish its scope.

Art.21(2)(a) risk analysis requires understanding the organisation's internal and external context; ISO 27001 Cl.4.1 requires the organisation to determine internal and external issues that are relevant to its purpose and affect the ISMS.

Art.21(2)(a) risk analysis includes considering obligations to interested parties; ISO 27001 Cl.4.2 requires the organisation to determine relevant interested parties and their requirements relevant to information security.
